risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used stratification at 2 levels: (1) use of antidepressants and (2) receiving treatment from a psychologist or psychiatrist at study entry. Block randomization was used with random blocks containing 4, 6, or 8 allocations. An independent researcher made the allocation schedule with a computerized random number generator and the investigators had no knowledge of the schedule." Comment: The study explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method: stratified block randomization with random block sizes, and the allocation schedule was created using a computerized random number generator. This supports truly random allocation.

Quote: "An independent researcher made the allocation schedule with a computerized random number generator and the investigators had no knowledge of the schedule. The participants were informed about randomization outcome via email." Comment: Although the paper does not describe central randomization or SNOSE, it explicitly states that the allocation schedule was held by an independent researcher and that the investigators had no knowledge of the schedule, which indicates that those enrolling participants could not foresee upcoming assignments before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The participants were informed about randomization outcome via email." and "Participants randomized to the CAU group received an email with the randomization outcome only..." and "Participants receive feedback on assignments from a coach." Comment: Participants clearly knew their assignment, and personnel delivering the intervention (coaches) also necessarily knew because they provided guided feedback. The main outcomes were largely self-reported and subjective (eg, "The primary outcome was depressive symptoms as measured by the CES-D," plus burnout, work performance, anxiety, and self-reported absenteeism), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes.

Quote: "The participants were informed about randomization outcome via email. Participants completed online questionnaires at baseline and posttreatment at 8 weeks (t1), 6 months (t2), and 12 months (t3)." Quote: "The primary outcome was depressive symptoms as measured by the CES-D..." Quote: "Burnout symptoms were measured with... This self-report questionnaire..." Quote: "The participant was asked to report the total number of days absent from work..." Quote: "Item 4 gives the best and easiest indication of the participant’s perception of their own work performance..." Comment: The main outcome assessors were the participants themselves (patient self-report) for depressive symptoms, burnout, absenteeism, work performance, anxiety, and health care utilization. Participants knew their allocation, and these outcomes are subjective or judgement-dependent, so knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. Although a structured telephone CIDI interview was also used, the study does not report blinded interviewers; under the worst-outcome rule, the unblinded self-reported subjective outcomes make this domain high risk overall.

Quote: "The attrition rates for the study sample were 26% at posttreatment assessment, 32% at the 6-month follow-up assessment, and 46% at the 12-month follow-up assessment. Participants in the CAU group completed the posttreatment assessment (χ2 1=11.5, P=.001) and the 6-month follow-up assessment (χ2 1=4.9, P=.03) more often." and "All analyses were performed according to the intention-to-treat (ITT) principle. Missing data were handled by multiple imputation via data augmentation." Comment: Post-randomization missing outcome data were substantial for the main repeated-measures outcomes, reaching 32% by 6 months and 46% by 12 months, with evidence of differential completion between groups at posttreatment and 6 months. Although ITT, LMM, and multiple imputation were used, the reported loss on the analyzed outcomes was high enough, and imbalance in completion was present, so attrition could plausibly bias results.
